## [00:00 - 03:00]

What constraint does a setter alleviate? Well, it considers alleviate two constraints. One is lead volume. So if you're not getting enough appointments, setters will help you get more appointments. Duh. The other one is lead efficiency, which is dollar per lead. So if your ROAS or profit margins aren't good, adding in setters is a good way to increase your dollar per lead, and then therefore, increase your margin for scale. So for example, if you're having trouble scaling because your return on ad spends three times, and that leaves you with a 20% profit margin based on your P&L, adding in a setter can get your ROAS to six times, and I don't know how that would reflect in the P&L. It probably is not going to be an exact like four. You're not going to go from 20% profit margin to 40%. But long story short, you might go to 40%. That might even get you all the way up to 50. And you're going to have, therefore, a much, much higher ceiling to scale based on your profit margin KPI. So a good way to think about setters is they help you get more appointments, but they just help you also get more appointments out of the same amount of ad spend.
